huntHessScale.description: Asymptomatic or minimal headache, slight nuchal rigidity
Moderate to severe headache, nuchal rigidity, no neurologic deficit except cranial nerve palsy
Stupor, moderate to severe hemiparesis, early decerebrate rigidity
Deep coma, decerebrate rigidity, moribund appearance

fisherScale.description: Diffuse deposition of blood, vertical layers < 1 mm thick
Localized clot or vertical layer > 1 mm thickness
Intracerebral or intraventricular clot with diffuse subarachnoid blood

WFNSScale.description: GCS 7-12, motor deficit present or absent
GCS 3-6, motor deficit present or absent

historyFeatures.typicalPresentation: Sudden-onset thunderclap headache reaching maximal intensity within seconds to minutes
historyFeatures.classicDescriptors: Worst headache of life, thunderclap headache, often described as explosive or like being hit on the head
associatedSymptoms: Transient or persistent loss of consciousness at onset
Focal neurologic deficits depending on aneurysm location
Third nerve palsy (posterior communicating artery aneurysm)
historyFeatures.sentinelHeadache: Small warning leak days to weeks prior to major rupture in up to 40% of patients
historyFeatures.onset: Sudden, during physical exertion, Valsalva maneuver, or emotional stress; can occur at rest

vitalSigns: Hypertension common due to sympathetic activation
May have bradycardia and hypertension (Cushing response if raised ICP)
generalExam: Brudzinski sign: neck flexion elicits hip and knee flexion
Kernig sign: knee extension limited with hip flexed
neurologicExam: Altered mental status ranging from confusion to coma
Third nerve palsy (dilated, unreactive pupil) with posterior communicating artery aneurysm
Sixth nerve palsy due to elevated intracranial pressure
Hemiparesis or aphasia if associated vasospasm or intracerebral hemorrhage
Vitreous hemorrhage (Terson syndrome) in severe cases

riskFactors: Saccular (berry) cerebral aneurysms - most common cause of spontaneous SAH
Family history of aneurysmal SAH or polycystic kidney disease
Connective tissue disorders: Ehlers-Danlos type IV, Marfan syndrome
Female sex, higher incidence with increasing age
African American and Japanese populations at increased risk

subtypes.name: Aneurysmal SAH
subtypes.description: Rupture of a saccular cerebral aneurysm, accounts for 85% of spontaneous SAH
subtypes.details: Most commonly at bifurcations of circle of Willis; anterior communicating artery, posterior communicating artery, middle cerebral artery bifurcation most frequent sites

subtypes.name: Perimesencephalic (nonaneurysmal) SAH
subtypes.description: Benign variant with blood confined to perimesencephalic cisterns, negative angiography
subtypes.details: Venous bleeding suspected; excellent prognosis, no vasospasm; requires repeat DSA to confirm absence of aneurysm

subtypes.name: Traumatic SAH
subtypes.description: Due to head trauma, blood in superficial sulci adjacent to fracture or contusion
subtypes.details: Management focuses on underlying brain injury; generally does not require aneurysm-specific treatment

subtypes.name: SAH from other vascular lesions
subtypes.description: Arteriovenous malformations, dural arteriovenous fistulas, spinal vascular malformations, intracranial arterial dissections
subtypes.details: Accounts for minority of cases; management directed at underlying lesion

mimics: Benign sexual headache or exertional headache

bedside: Serial neurologic examinations for vasospasm monitoring
Vital sign monitoring including blood pressure control
Electrocardiogram: assess for neurogenic stunned myocardium, arrhythmias, T-wave inversions, QT prolongation
laboratory: Complete blood count: leukocytosis common, baseline for nimodipine monitoring
Serum electrolytes: monitor for hyponatremia (SIADH or cerebral salt wasting)
Coagulation profile: PT/PTT/INR to rule out coagulopathy
Troponin: assess for neurogenic myocardial injury
Arterial blood gas: if altered mental status or respiratory concerns
Type and screen: in preparation for potential surgical intervention
Serum osmolality and urine studies: differentiate SIADH from cerebral salt wasting

imaging.sensitivity: >98% within 6 hours, 93% within 24 hours, decreases thereafter
imaging.findings: Hyperdense blood in subarachnoid cisterns, may localize to specific cisterns suggesting aneurysm site
Identifies aneurysm location, size, neck morphology, relation to parent vessels; may show additional unruptured aneurysms

imaging.timing: Gold standard, typically within 24h if CTA inconclusive
imaging.sensitivity: Highest sensitivity, defines vascular anatomy precisely
imaging.findings: Definitive aneurysm characterization; three-vessel or four-vessel study; may require repeat if initial negative

imaging.timing: If CT negative but high clinical suspicion
imaging.findings: Xanthochromia (bilirubin) by spectrophotometry; elevated RBC count not clearing from tube 1 to tube 4

imaging.timing: Alternative if CT delayed >24h or negative
imaging.findings: High FLAIR signal in subarachnoid spaces; GRE shows susceptibility artifact from blood breakdown products

imaging.timing: Daily or every other day starting day 1-3 for vasospasm surveillance
imaging.findings: Elevated mean flow velocities in MCA >120 cm/s suggest vasospasm; Lindegaard ratio >3 confirms vasospasm

management.protocolReference: AHA/ASA Guidelines for Management of Aneurysmal Subarachnoid Hemorrhage (Stroke, updated 2023)
immediateManagement: Airway, breathing, circulation assessment and stabilization
Transfer to intensive care unit and tertiary neurovascular center
Blood pressure control: systolic <160 mm Hg until aneurysm secured (nicardipine or labetalol)
Avoid hypotension after aneurysm secured to prevent delayed cerebral ischemia

nimodipine.dose: 60 mg orally or via nasogastric tube every 4 hours
nimodipine.duration: Continue for 21 days after SAH onset
nimodipine.mechanism: Calcium channel blocker, reduces incidence of poor outcomes and delayed cerebral ischemia
nimodipine.monitoring: Monitor for hypotension; reduce dose if significant hypotension occurs

aneurysmTreatment.endovascularCoiling: Preferred for posterior circulation aneurysms, narrow-necked aneurysms, and many anterior circulation aneurysms; involves placing platinum coils into aneurysm sac via microcatheter
aneurysmTreatment.surgicalClipping: Preferred for middle cerebral artery aneurysms, wide-necked aneurysms, large intraparenchymal hematomas requiring evacuation; craniotomy with clip application across aneurysm neck
aneurysmTreatment.flowDiversion: Stent placement across wide-necked or fusiform aneurysms to divert flow and induce thrombosis
aneurysmTreatment.timing: Early treatment within 24-72 hours of onset to prevent rebleeding and facilitate vasospasm management

vasospasmManagement: Maintain euvolemia or mild hypervolemia (avoid prophylactic hypervolemia)
Induce hypertension if symptomatic vasospasm develops: systolic 160-200 mm Hg
Hemodilution only if hematocrit <25%, otherwise maintain normovolemia
Balloon angioplasty for proximal vessel vasospasm refractory to medical therapy
Oral or IV statins may reduce incidence of vasospasm
supportiveCare: Elevate head of bed 30 degrees to promote cerebral venous drainage
Seizure prophylaxis: levetiracetam or phenytoin for short-term use
Fever control: normothermia maintenance, avoid hyperthermia which worsens outcomes
Glucose control: maintain normoglycemia, avoid both hypo- and hyperglycemia
Hyponatremia management: volume repletion, fludrocortisone for cerebral salt wasting; fluid restriction contraindicated
Deep vein thrombosis prophylaxis with sequential compression devices; heparin after aneurysm secured
pathways: Pre-securing: systolic BP <160 mmHg, Nimodipine 60mg q4h, urgent CTA/DSA, neurosurgical consultation
Post-securing: ICU monitoring, TCD surveillance daily, maintain normovolemia, monitor for vasospasm, treat delayed cerebral ischemia
Rehabilitation: multidisciplinary team including physical therapy, occupational therapy, speech therapy, neuropsychological assessment

pathophysiology.primaryMechanism: Rupture of a cerebral aneurysm causes sudden extravasation of arterial blood into the subarachnoid space
pathophysiology.hemodynamicEffects: Sudden increase in intracranial pressure; ICP may briefly approach systolic arterial pressure causing transient cerebral perfusion arrest and loss of consciousness
acutePhase: Global cerebral ischemia from raised ICP and reduced CPP
Neurogenic stunned myocardium from catecholamine surge
Neurogenic pulmonary edema from sympathetic overactivity
subacutePhase: Delayed cerebral ischemia from vasospasm: arterial narrowing due to smooth muscle contraction and inflammatory changes, typically days 4-14
Microthrombosis and cortical spreading depolarizations contribute to delayed ischemia
Inflammatory response: blood breakdown products release hemoglobin, endothelin-1, and free radicals promoting vasoconstriction
Blood-brain barrier disruption and cerebral edema
chronicPhase: Hydrocephalus: acute (within hours) or chronic (days to weeks) from impaired CSF absorption by blood products

complications: Rebleeding: highest risk in first 24 hours, dramatically reduced by early aneurysm treatment; mortality >50%
Delayed cerebral ischemia from vasospasm: peak incidence day 5-14, leading cause of morbidity and mortality after successful aneurysm treatment
Hydrocephalus: acute, subacute, or chronic; may require external ventricular drain or later ventriculoperitoneal shunt
Hyponatremia: from cerebral salt wasting (CSW) or SIADH; differentiating critical for management (CSW requires volume repletion, SIADH fluid restriction)
Seizures: 10-20% incidence, usually within first 24 hours
Cardiac complications: neurogenic stunned myocardium (Takotsubo-like), arrhythmias, myocardial injury
Cerebral salt wasting: hyponatremia with hypovolemia, high urine sodium, high urine osmolality
mustNotMiss: Thunderclap headache is a medical emergency requiring immediate CT head STAT
Do NOT perform LP without CT first if SAH suspected due to risk of herniation
Negative CT within 6 hours does not rule out SAH; proceed with lumbar puncture for xanthochromia
Do not delay nimodipine while awaiting aneurysm treatment - start immediately
Avoid hypotension before aneurysm securing and avoid hypovolemia at all phases
Do not administer anticoagulation or antiplatelet therapy before aneurysm is secured
Sentinel headache may precede catastrophic rupture by days to weeks
emergencyPriority: CRITICAL - Immediate neuroimaging and neurosurgical consultation required
diagnosticClues: Thunderclap headache: maximal intensity within seconds, unprecedented severity, classic 'worst headache of life'
Neck stiffness developing within hours of headache onset
Loss of consciousness at headache onset highly suggestive
CT hyperdensity in basilar cisterns is diagnostic
Xanthochromia on LP spectrophotometry is diagnostic if CT negative
Third nerve palsy with ipsilateral headache suggests posterior communicating artery aneurysm
Onset during Valsalva maneuver, exertion, or sexual activity
exclusionClues: Gradual onset headache evolving over hours is more consistent with migraine or meningitis
Positional headache worse upright better recumbent suggests intracranial hypotension
Recurrent thunderclap headaches over days to weeks suggests RCVS rather than aneurysmal SAH
Normal CT and normal LP effectively excludes SAH
Nonaneurysmal perimesencephalic pattern on CT suggests benign variant
